item.title: Young householder, wandering the streets at improper times has six dangers: oneself is unprotected and unguarded, one's wife and children are unprotected and unguarded, one's…
item.description:
Young householder, wandering the streets at improper times has six dangers: oneself is unprotected and unguarded, one's wife and children are unprotected and unguarded, one's property is unprotected and unguarded, one is suspected of evil deeds, one is subject to false rumors, and one encounters many troubles. Young householder, such are the six dangers of wandering the streets at improper times.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 31 – Siṅgālasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Nāgita, for those who are heavy with craving for pleasure, Nāgita, with the change and alteration of that, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise.…
item.description:
Nāgita, for those who are heavy with craving for pleasure, Nāgita, with the change and alteration of that, sorrow, lamentation, pain, grief, and despair arise. That is the natural consequence for that person. For those who focus their minds on the sign of the foul, Nāgita, disenchantment with the sign of the beautiful becomes established. That is the natural consequence for that person. For one who dwells contemplating impermanence in the six sense-spheres, Nāgita, disenchantment with contact becomes established. That is the natural consequence for that person. For one who dwells contemplating arising and passing away in the five aggregates of clinging, Nāgita, disenchantment with clinging becomes established. That is the natural consequence for that person.

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.30
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Mahā Moggallāna: We are forest dwellers, Living on almsfood, Content with the food Discarded in the bowl. We crush Māra and his army, If…
item.description:
Elder Mahā Moggallāna:

We are forest dwellers,
Living on almsfood,
Content with the food
Discarded in the bowl.
We crush Māra and his army,
If the mind is well concentrated.

We are forest dwellers,
Living on almsfood,
Content with the food
Discarded in the bowl.
We drive away Māra and his army,
Like an elephant [crushes] a hut of reeds.

We dwell at the foot of a tree,
Patient, with persistent mind,
Content with the food
Discarded in the bowl.
We crush Māra and his army,
If the mind is well concentrated.

We dwell at the foot of a tree,
Patient, with persistent mind,
Content with the food
Discarded in the bowl.
We drive away Māra and his army,
Like an elephant [crushes] a hut of reeds.

From: Theragāthā 20.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, any samaṇas or brahmins who are not bound, not infatuated, not obsessed with these five strands of sensual pleasure, see the danger in them,…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, any samaṇas or brahmins who are not bound, not infatuated, not obsessed with these five strands of sensual pleasure, see the danger in them, know the escape from them, and enjoy them — these should be understood as: 'They have not fallen into misfortune, not into calamity, and are not being used by Māra as he wishes.' Bhikkhus, just as a forest deer that has not fallen into a trap lies down — that deer should be understood as: 'It has not fallen into misfortune, not into calamity, and is not being used by the hunter as he wishes. When the hunter comes, the deer can leave as it wishes.' So too, bhikkhus, those samaṇas or brahmins who are not bound ... are not being used by Māra as he wishes.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 26 – Ariyapariyesanāsutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: And, bhikkhus, what is right view? Bhikkhus, knowledge of suffering, knowledge of the origin of suffering, knowledge of the cessation of suffering, knowledge of the…
item.description:
And, bhikkhus, what is right view? Bhikkhus, knowledge of suffering, knowledge of the origin of suffering, knowledge of the cessation of suffering, knowledge of the path leading to the cessation of suffering. Bhikkhus, this is called right view. And, bhikkhus, what is right intention? Bhikkhus, intention of renunciation, intention of non-ill-will, intention of harmlessness. Bhikkhus, this is called right intention. And, bhikkhus, what is right speech? Bhikkhus, abstaining from false speech, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, abstaining from idle chatter. Bhikkhus, this is called right speech. And, bhikkhus, what is right action? Bhikkhus, abstaining from killing living beings, abstaining from taking what is not given, abstaining from sexual misconduct. Bhikkhus, this is called right action. And, bhikkhus, what is right livelihood? Here, bhikkhus, a noble disciple, having abandoned wrong livelihood, earns his living by right livelihood. Bhikkhus, this is called right livelihood.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 45.8
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: And, Puṇṇa, what is neither-dark-nor-bright action with a neither-dark-nor-bright result, action leading to the cessation of action? Here, Puṇṇa, the volition (cetanā) that abandons dark…
item.description:
And, Puṇṇa, what is neither-dark-nor-bright action with a neither-dark-nor-bright result, action leading to the cessation of action? Here, Puṇṇa, the volition (cetanā) that abandons dark action with its dark result, the volition that abandons bright action with its bright result, the volition that abandons dark-and-bright action with its dark-and-bright result — this, Puṇṇa, is called neither-dark-nor-bright action with a neither-dark-nor-bright result, action leading to the cessation of action. Puṇṇa, these four kinds of action have been directly known, realized, and proclaimed by me.

From: Majjhima Nikāya 57 – Kukkuravatikasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Bhikkhus, the eye is impermanent, changing, altering. The ear is impermanent, changing, altering. The nose is impermanent, changing, altering. The tongue is impermanent, changing, altering.…
item.description:
Bhikkhus, the eye is impermanent, changing, altering. The ear is impermanent, changing, altering. The nose is impermanent, changing, altering. The tongue is impermanent, changing, altering. The body is impermanent, changing, altering. The mind is impermanent, changing, altering. Bhikkhus, one who has faith and conviction in these things is called a faith-follower, who has entered the fixed course of rightness, entered the plane of true persons, transcended the plane of ordinary persons. He cannot do any action that would cause him to be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm; he cannot pass away without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. One who, with wisdom, has a degree of acceptance of these things is called a Dhamma-follower, who has entered the fixed course of rightness, entered the plane of true persons, transcended the plane of ordinary persons. He cannot do any action that would cause him to be reborn in hell, the animal realm, or the ghost realm; he cannot pass away without realizing the fruit of stream-entry. One who knows and sees these things as they are is called a stream-enterer, no longer subject to falling, bound for enlightenment.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 25.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: 'Friend, these five faculties have different domains, different ranges, and they do not experience one another's domain and range. That is, the eye, ear, nose,…
item.description:
'Friend, these five faculties have different domains, different ranges, and they do not experience one another's domain and range. That is, the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body. Friend, since these five faculties have different domains, different ranges, and do not experience one another's domain and range, what is their support, and what experiences their domain and range?' — 'Friend, these five faculties have different domains, different ranges, and they do not experience one another's domain and range, namely, the eye, ear, nose, tongue, body. Friend, among these five faculties, the mind is their support, and the mind experiences their domain and range.'

From: Majjhima Nikāya 43 – Mahāvedallasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: Elder Cūḷaka: Peacocks call, beautiful-crested, with lovely tail feathers, Their necks bright blue, beaks beautiful, voices melodious. This land is well covered with grass, well…
item.description:
Elder Cūḷaka:

Peacocks call, beautiful-crested, with lovely tail feathers,
Their necks bright blue, beaks beautiful, voices melodious.
This land is well covered with grass, well moistened with water,
Well covered by the sky, well covered by rain clouds.

The human body is well healthy, the mind good, practicing jhāna.
Excellent is the well-arisen mind in the Buddha's well-spoken teaching.
Experience the path leading to the Deathless,
That path is unsurpassed, the pure, healthy,
Subtle, fine path, well seen.

From: Theragāthā 2.46
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: - Poṭṭhapāda, there are three kinds of self-identification: the gross self-identification, the mind-made self-identification, and the formless self-identification. Poṭṭhapāda, what is the gross self-identification? Having…
item.description:
- Poṭṭhapāda, there are three kinds of self-identification: the gross self-identification, the mind-made self-identification, and the formless self-identification. Poṭṭhapāda, what is the gross self-identification? Having form, composed of the four great elements, nourished by solid food — this is the gross self-identification. What is the mind-made self-identification? Having form, mind-made, complete with all major and minor parts, with all faculties intact — this is the mind-made self-identification. What is the formless self-identification? Formless, made of perception — this is the formless self-identification.

From: Dīgha Nikāya 9 – Poṭṭhapādasutta
📖 Read this sutta
